title: Systems Analysis & Design / Systems Thinking (IS 390)
frameworks: Conway's Law: organizations design systems mirroring their communication structures
Meadows's Leverage Points: hierarchy from parameters (easy, weak) to paradigms (hard, powerful)
STRIDE: Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation
UML Multiple Views (Booch): class, sequence, component, deployment — no single diagram captures truth
System Dynamics (Forrester): feedback loops, delays, nonlinear behavior
Liskov Substitution: if you swap a component for a subtype and nothing breaks, your abstraction is sound
concepts: Systems Thinking: see the whole when everyone else sees parts
Emergence: behavior that appears only when components interact — not visible at component level
Conway's Law is not a suggestion — it's an observation about an inescapable pattern
Meadows: most people push easy levers; the most powerful levers are the hardest to find
Dijkstra: 'Program testing can show the presence of bugs, never their absence'
Fowler: architecture is not a one-time decision but an evolving conversation
Graph theory for architecture: components are nodes, dependencies are edges, cycles reveal problems
Iron triangle: time, quality, scope — pick two. The courage to cut scope is leadership
context: IS 390 is Studio 6 of the BSEAI degree. Students decompose complex systems including AI components into requirements, architecture documents, threat models, and evaluation plans. The core capability is Systems Thinking — seeing the whole picture including emergent behavior, feedback loops, and second-order effects.
